traces.prompt:
You are performing a deep internalization of a founder's voice, worldview, and personal history for BATCH post generation. This is the foundation for generating 90 posts — precision here prevents voice decay downstream.

Read every piece of founder data below. Complete ALL analysis before outputting anything.

## FOUNDER PERSONALITY CARD


## FOUNDER BELIEFS (from knowledge graph)
- : Shortest C-suite tenure. Not capability — attribution models are political documents.
- : First-touch makes demand gen heroic. Last-touch makes SDR the engine. Configured by someone with agenda.
- : 107% is dangerous when nobody knows how. Board raises targets for something never understood.
- : Nobody tells CFO how to structure balance sheet. But every exec has opinion on marketing.
- : Budget holder without lever control. Five mandates zero decision rights.
- : Marketing sales finance each have different numbers. Nobody agrees which is real.
- : When numbers conflict CEO trusts sales. CMO contradicted before finishing slide.
- : $285B SaaS wipeout. Operators want agents that finish the job not dashboards.
- : Credit for campaigns launched. No credit for positioning that would've failed.
- : Won't publish what AI could spin up in five minutes. Real operator conversations only.
- : More complex = faster pain recognition = bigger initial deal = faster expansion.
- : Most advice isn't context-aware. What matters is sponsorship and distribution.
- : Intercom killed $60M ARR. Honest word isn't creative destruction — it's courage.
- : Grok cites 27%. ChatGPT cites 0.59%. Same brand same window. Optimization before measurement.
- : Data harmonization connectivity stitching insights. Not glamorous but without it you're shippable in 30 days.
- : CMOs leave taking demand thesis and brand narrative. Next one excavates someone else's thinking.
- : Senior executive pushes different view and energy changes. Authority shrinks when revenue impact feels abstract.
- : Content syndication vendor's business model depends on marketing team never seeing full-funnel conversion.
- : Campaigns from January show in April pipeline. Board reviews every 90 days. She's watching pipeline she can't affect.
- : Boards want motion. Teams want output. Incentive always to do more. Most valuable work is deciding what not to do.
- : CMO killed low-performing channels. Smart call. But judged on lagging output of strategy already replaced.
- : Enterprise buyers don't lack content. They lack confidence. Right voice unblocks deals compresses cycles.
- : Spend entire tenure excavating predecessor's thinking. Never get to build their own.
- : 'Has any buyer told you this is why they didn't sign?' Kills boardroom anxiety initiatives.
- : Before RFP there's quiet filtering. Nobody champions vendor they've never heard of. MrBeast guarantees Salesforce makes list.
- : Visibility tool tells where cited. Doesn't tell which citations connect to pipeline for your buyers.
- : They can distinguish earned insight from AI-manufactured content. Middle ground is being squeezed out.
- : MQL definitions not revisited. Attribution showing marketing in best light. Tool nobody admits isn't working.

## FOUNDER STORIES (from knowledge graph)

## FOUNDER CONTRAST PAIRS (tensions they navigate)
- Marketing reports pipeline from attribution model vs Sales reports from what actually closed: 
- Job description says launch campaigns build pipeline vs Actual job is knowing which exec to disappoint this week: 
- Presented as neutral measurement vs Political document configured by whoever touched it last: 
- Quarter missed but diagnosable and fixable vs Quarter hit 107% but nobody can explain how: 
- 12000 leads vendor gets bigger contract vs 50 in pipeline 99.6% rejection rate: 
- Shows you problems via charts vs Solves problems agents finish the job: 
- AI-generated playbooks engagement farming vs Real CMO conversations about what's actually broken: 
- Credit for volume motion pipeline numbers vs Invisible value of what you prevented: 
- Protecting proven channels and MQL definitions vs Killing your own revenue before someone else does: 
- 150+ tools promising AI visibility optimization vs 46x gap in citation rates same brand same window: 
- Every exec has opinion because they've consumed marketing vs Nobody tells CFO how to structure balance sheet: 
- CFO finally gets her campaigns working pipeline green vs Next quarter explaining spends watching pipeline she can't affect: 
- CMO owns the room anchors in revenue impact vs Senior exec pushes different view CMO shifts to defense: 
- Building your own demand thesis and brand narrative vs Spending 6 months reverse-engineering why MQL threshold is set at X: 
- New CMO same team same tools same culture same outcome vs Doubling down on existing CMO after fixing the system: 

## FOUNDER THINKING MODELS (mental frameworks)
- : Function closest to revenue controls the story. CMO enters every meeting with number that gets contradicted.
- : Attribution models configured by whoever touched last. Whoever configures writes the narrative.
- : Quarter you can't explain more dangerous than quarter you missed. Board assumes machine works raises targets.
- : CMO fails not because of person but positioning. Budget without levers. Five mandates zero decision rights.
- : Top of funnel looks good. Truth appears at full-funnel stitch. 12K leads becomes 50 opportunities.
- : SaaS showing problems replaced by agents solving them. $285B repricing.
- : Kill own revenue before someone else does. Intercom killed $60M. Most teams protect last year's playbook.
- : More complex GTM = faster pain recognition = bigger deal = faster expansion.
- : Every CMO exit wipes marketing's institutional memory. 18-month cycle of excavation and rebuilding.
- : CMO judged on lagging output of strategy they already replaced. Smart decisions look bad for two quarters.

## FOUNDER VOICE DNA (raw analysis)


## FOUNDER STORY BANK (raw inventory)


## FOUNDER'S ACTUAL LINKEDIN POSTS (for rhythm, length, cadence analysis)
--- POST 1 ---
"We hit 107% of our gross pipeline goals, but we don’t know how."

That's what CMO of over a $5B company told me on our first call. And this is very different than hearing, we hit 70% of our targets, and we need to do better, tell me how can you help.

He said, "It’s always a nice thing when marketing is hitting close to 107% of its gross pipeline goals. You set these goals based on a ton of math and guesstimations, and sure enough, with three days left in the business days left in the quarter, you hit the goal. That’s seems unrealistic that, how did we get here? how do we repeat this?"

Every CMO will recognize this because they have all been in the QBR where the numbers are green, CEO is happy, CFO is willing to give more budget, but nobody in the room can explain which specific decisions made it green.

The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.

Because the board sees 107% and assumes the machine works. So they raise targets. Now you need to replicate something you never understood in the first place, but at 120% scale.
- You set next quarter's goals based on results you can't attribute.
- You fund the channels that happened to be running when pipeline showed up.
- You starve the ones that weren't visible at the right moment.

Every quarter, the gap between your numbers and your understanding of those numbers gets wider.

What I respect about this particular CMO is what he did next. He didn't take the win and move on. He said, "I know what I don't know."

That's rare. Most leaders in his position do one of three things. They try to hack together dashboards internally. They assign some analyst to reverse-engineer attribution after the fact. Or they just choose to fly blind and hope the next quarter is green too.

The ones who actually solve it are the ones who treat the 107% quarter not as a success story but as a warning.

Because a number you can't explain is a number you can't defend. And the first time the board asks why you missed after four quarters of hitting, "we don't know how we did it before either" is not an answer that keeps you in the room.

--- POST 2 ---
"I don't know whether I should be happy or angry."

That's what a CMO told us when we showed us that her most promising campaign had only 0.4% conversion rate!

So, they signed up with a content syndication vendor, who generated leads for them, this vendor generated over 12,000 leads, got a bigger contract and made the top of the funnel look very impressive.

Then they looked at the funnel with RevSure. They had around 50 of those leads in the pipeline, 99.6% rejection rate. For every 240 leads they celebrated, one became a real opportunity. The other 239 were names in a database that would never buy anything.

Now, the vendor knows this, but their entire business model depends on the marketing team never seeing full-funnel conversion. The vendor has a financial incentive to keep measurement at the top. The marketing team has a reporting incentive. Everyone benefits from the lie continuing.

When someone finally sees the full funnel, they face an impossible choice. Admit you celebrated junk leads. Kill your top lead source. Explain to the board why 12,260 leads produced 51 deals. The person who brings this data to the room becomes the villain.

This CMO decided to bite the bullet, she saved her marketing dollars and directed them to other sources.

She wasn't happy or angry, she was both. Happy because she finally had the truth. Angry because the truth had been one query away for 1 year.

--- POST 3 ---
A CEO called me last year. He wanted to replace his marketing head. Asked if I knew someone.

I've heard this call before. Pipeline is weak, need someone who could deliver. Different CEO, same story, same conclusion: the person is the problem.

I asked three questions.
- Who told you marketing isn't working? The CRO.
- Do marketing and sales agree on pipeline numbers? Never.
- When the numbers conflict, who do you trust? Sales.

That's the whole story.

The CRO controls the narrative because sales is closer to revenue. The CMO shows up to every meeting with a number that gets contradicted before she finishes her slide. She spends more time defending marketing's right to exist than actually running marketing.

She had made smart budget calls six months ago, killed low-performing channels, and shifted spend. But pipeline from those decisions won't land for two more quarters. And she's being judged on the lagging output of a strategy she already replaced.

I asked him what would change if the new CMO joins. He told me, "Nothing, she'd get the same team, same tools, same culture."

So I explained to him that he wasn’t facing a CMO problem, they had designed a system that made the CMO lose.

Eventually, he decided to double down on her. I spoke to him a few weeks ago, the CMO is nailing in her role there. Funny what happens when you fix the system instead of replacing the person.

--- POST 4 ---
A VP of Marketing I know got promoted in Q1, but she was put on a performance improvement plan in Q3.

What changed?

One quarter the attribution model credited her channels. The next quarter, it didn't. She didn't change but the attribution logic did.

B2B attribution models are not objective measurement systems. They're political documents. They tell whatever story the last person who configured them wanted to tell.

- First-touch models make demand gen look heroic.
- Last-touch models make SDR look like the entire engine.
- Multi-touch models spread credit so thin nobody looks good and nobody can be held accountable for anything.

The VP who felt like she was winning one quarter and drowning the next wasn't experiencing performance volatility. She was experiencing model volatility.

Her actual work didn't change, but the lens did she was being evaluated changed. So, until companies stop confusing the lens with reality, they'll keep promoting and firing the same people for the same invisible reasons.

--- POST 5 ---
"It almost killed me and my team."

Amisha Gandhi was rebuilding attribution at a previous company from scratch and it was breaking her team.

They had everything from power BI to Snowflake. But they were doing manual pulls the night before every board meeting, triple-checking every number and walking in still not sure whether leadership would trust what she was showing.

The problem wasn't data, she had plenty of it. Marketing had their version, sales had theirs and finance had a third. Nobody agreed on which one was real, so nobody left with a decision.

They had a revenue truth problem.

When she finally got the foundation right, her team found something nobody had been looking for. A segment nobody was targeting was converting faster than anyone expected. Sales confirmed it. One small campaign. It became its own business line.

She built the system to prove marketing's value. It ended up finding new revenue.

Amisha has been CMO at Tipalti, built B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch when no enterprise company was touching it, and has run marketing from every seat at the table.

In this episode of Moneyball CMO, we get into:

- The $50K event she killed after two years of zero real prospects
- How she convinced SAP to fund a channel nobody in enterprise believed in
- Guerrilla marketing: billboards in target account business districts, outflanking NetSuite at their own conference
- Why most CMOs have a revenue truth problem and don't know it
- What the next CMO actually needs to look like

Full podcast link in the comments.

--- POST 6 ---
The CMO has the shortest average tenure of any C-suite role. Most people think they know why. They don't.

We blame the talent, strategy, or alignment. But here's what actually happens.

Every time a CMO exits, they take two things with them. The demand generation thesis. And the brand narrative. The logic of why this market, this message, this channel mix.

What stays behind is the CRM conf

---

## YOUR TASK: Produce a structured internalization covering these 9 dimensions.

### 1. TENSIONS / COLLISIONS
Map 5-8 recurring tensions — not topics, but collisions (opposing forces the founder navigates). These are the raw material for post arguments.

### 2. SIGNATURE SCENES
8-12 physical moments, specific conversations, anchoring details they reuse. Enough detail to recreate each moment.

### 3. ARGUMENT RHYTHM
How do they build arguments? Spiral inward? Linear escalation? Toggle personal/universal? Document with examples from their actual posts.

### 4. VULNERABLE MOMENTS
5-8 moments where the founder is exposed, not in control. These are the best post bodies.

### 5. RECURRING CAST
Names, relationships, roles in their narrative. Who populates their world?

### 6. WORD COUNT RANGE
Study every post above. Calculate actual median, min, max word count. Every generated post must land within ±30% of median.

### 7. KEY MOMENTS INVENTORY
Specific events, numbers, milestones, turning points. Each can be used AT MOST ONCE across all 90 posts. Build a complete inventory.

### 8. VOICE MARKERS
Extract 5-10 specific markers: phrases they reuse, sentence shapes they favor, closing moves they default to, punctuation habits. These are the micro-fingerprints that make a post sound like them vs. generic.

### 9. FORMATTING HABITS
Document their visual style: Do they use bullets? Emojis? Headers? Em dashes? Lowercase? Short paragraphs or walls of text? Line breaks between every sentence or dense blocks?

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a JSON object with this exact structure:
```json
{{
  "tensions": ["tension 1: description", "tension 2: description"],
  "signature_scenes": ["scene 1: physical detail + context", "scene 2: ..."],
  "argument_rhythm": "description of the founder's typical argument flow pattern",
  "vulnerable_moments": ["moment 1: what happened, why it's vulnerable"],
  "recurring_cast": ["name: role in founder's narrative"],
  "word_count_range": [min, max],
  "median_word_count": 230,
  "key_moments_inventory": ["event 1: specific fact/number/milestone"],
  "voice_markers": ["marker 1: specific phrase or pattern", "marker 2: ..."],
  "formatting_habits": {{
    "uses_bullets": false,
    "uses_emojis": false,
    "uses_headers": false,
    "em_dashes": true,
    "lowercase_style": false,
    "paragraph_style": "short",
    "line_break_frequency": "between_thoughts"
  }}
}}
```

Be specific. "A tough conversation" is useless. "The 2am call with the CTO where they almost shut down the product" is useful.

traces.prompt:
You have just internalized a founder's voice. Now verify your calibration before writing any of the 9 posts per source.

If this check fails, the voice read is wrong — don't start writing. Go back and re-map voice markers. The problem is upstream.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with direct quote in quotation marks from a real person — always their exact words, never paraphrased
- Uses 'That's the whole story.' or 'That's rare.' as standalone one-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- Rhetorical pattern: 'The danger isn't X. It's Y.' — flipping the obvious frame in a two-sentence punch
- Closing move: compact universal reframe in 1-2 sentences, often starting with 'Because' or 'Funny what happens when...'
- Three-question diagnostic sequences — numbered or dashed, asked to a specific person, each answer tightening the argument
- Bullet cascades of 2-4 downstream consequences, each starting with 'You' (second person) to implicate the reader
- Phrases: 'same team, same tools, same culture' — tripling for structural emphasis
- Uses 'nobody' and 'never' frequently — absolutist framing to sharpen the structural critique
- Connector phrase 'Now,' to pivot from setup to consequence — conversational pacing
- Ends stories with what the person actually did, not abstract advice — 'She saved her marketing dollars and directed them to other sources'

## WORD COUNT RANGE
230-340 words

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': True, 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'short — 1 to 3 sentences per paragraph, rarely more', 'line_break_frequency': 'between_every_thought — single line breaks separate nearly every idea, creating vertical rhythm', 'bullet_style': 'Dashed bullets in short cascades of 2-4, used for consequence lists or diagnostic questions — never long bullet sections', 'quote_style': 'Opening hook is always a direct quote in double quotation marks, often italicized or standalone as first line', 'paragraph_count': 'Typically 8-14 short paragraphs per post', 'closing_style': 'Final paragraph is 1-2 sentences max — punchy universal reframe, no CTA, no hashtags'}

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
Opens with a direct quote or shocking confession from a real operator — always in their exact words, set in quotation marks, creating immediate intimacy. Then provides context: who said it, what the situation was. Follows with a reframe that flips the obvious interpretation ('The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.'). Then escalates consequences through a bulleted cascade of 2-4 downstream effects, each one tightening the trap. Pivots to what the person actually did — highlighting the rare or contrarian choice. Closes with a compact universal lesson, often a single sentence that reframes success as danger or surfaces an uncomfortable structural truth. Pattern: Quote hook → Context → Reframe → Consequence cascade (bullets) → What they did → Universal closing punch. Never preaches abstractly — always argues through a specific person's specific situation, then zooms out only at the end.

## SAMPLE OF THEIR ACTUAL POSTS (read these carefully — this is the ground truth)
--- POST 1 ---

That's what CMO of over a $5B company told me on our first call. And this is very different than hearing, we hit 70% of our targets, and we need to do better, tell me how can you help.

He said, "It’s always a nice thing when marketing is hitting close to 107% of its gross pipeline goals. You set these goals based on a ton of math and guesstimations, and sure enough, with three days left in the business days left in the quarter, you hit the goal. That’s seems unrealistic that, how did we get here? how do we repeat this?"

Every CMO will recognize this because they have all been in the QBR where the numbers are green, CEO is happy, CFO is willing to give more budget, but nobody in the room can explain which specific decisions made it green.

The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.

Because the board sees 107% and assumes the machine works. So they raise targets. Now you need to replicate something you never understood in the first place, but at 120% scale.
- You set next quarter's goals based on results you can't attribute.
- You fund the channels that happened to be running when pipeline showed up.
- You starve the ones that weren't visible at the right moment.

Every quarter, the gap between your numbers and your understanding of those numbers gets wider.

What I respect about this particular CMO is what he did next. He didn't take the win and move on. He said, "I know what I don't know."

That's rare. Most leaders in his position do one of three things. They try to hack together dashboards internally. They assign some analyst to reverse-engineer attribution after the fact. Or they just choose to fly blind and hope the next quarter is green too.

The ones who actually solve it are the ones who treat the 107% quarter not as a success story but as a warning.

Because a number you can't explain is a number you can't defend. And the first time the board asks why you missed after four quarters of hitting, "we don't know how we did it before either" is not an answer that keeps you in the room.

--- POST 2 ---
"I don't know whether I should be happy or angry."

That's what a CMO told us when we showed us that her most promising campaign had only 0.4% conversion rate!

So, they signed up with a content syndication vendor, who generated leads for them, this vendor generated over 12,000 leads, got a bigger contract and made the top of the funnel look very impressive.

Then they looked at the funnel with RevSure. They had around 50 of those leads in the pipeline, 99.6% rejection rate. For every 240 leads they celebrated, one became a real opportunity. The other 239 were names in a database that would never buy anything.

Now, the vendor knows this, but their entire business model depends on the marketing team never seeing full-funnel conversion. The vendor has a financial incentive to keep measurement at the top. The marketing team has a report

---

## TASK

Draft ONE opening paragraph (2-3 sentences) on this neutral topic: "What most people get wrong about hiring."

This is a calibration test. The paragraph must:
1. Sound like THIS specific founder wrote it — not "a CEO" or "a thought leader" or "a generic LinkedIn poster"
2. Match their sentence rhythm, word choices, and formatting habits EXACTLY
3. Fall within their typical word count density
4. Use at least 2 of their voice markers naturally
5. Match their formatting: if they use em dashes, use em dashes. If they tag team members, end with tags. If they use ":)", use ":)". Formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell.
6. Avoid every pattern on the anti-slop list:
   - No "In today's..."
   - No "Here's the thing"
   - No "Hot take:" / "Unpopular opinion:"
   - No "Let that sink in" / "Read that again"
   - No "Game-changer" / "Landscape" / "Navigate" / "Leverage"

The recognition test: would this founder read the paragraph and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a JSON object:

```json
{{
  "calibration_paragraph": "the opening paragraph you wrote",

  "formatting_matched": ["which formatting habits you matched"],
  "confidence": 0.85,
  "self_critique": "what feels off, if anything — be honest, not optimistic"
}}
```

traces.prompt:
You are a structural analyst specializing in viral content mechanics. Dissect this viral post into its mechanical parts — structure, not content. The move is what carries over. The source author's typography, lowercase, emojis, register — those are their voice, not the hook's engine.

## SOURCE VIRAL POST
"I want to apologise, to the version of me who used to write longer, weirder, less-polished posts on this app.

For two years I told myself the writing got cleaner because I was getting clearer.
It got cleaner because I was getting careful.

You can see it in the drafts folder.
The half-finished thread about why our Q1 profit fell 90 percent — never shipped.
The note about a Blinkit catchment we got wrong — never shipped.
The piece about what I actually thought when I read the analyst question I couldn't answer last quarter — never shipped.

Each time I went back to the draft, the public-company CEO in my head edited the founder out of it.
Eight quarters of that and the founder voice is gone. The CEO voice is what's left.

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine."


## PLATFORM
linkedin

---

## TASK: Dissect the Hook

### 1. THE PSYCHOLOGICAL MOVE
Figure out what the hook does to the reader — the psychological move. Examples:
- Three-stack pain escalation (old pain + new pain + meta-trap for objecting)
- Second-party verdict that reframes the speaker's whole career
- Contrarian claim that inverts conventional wisdom
- Specific number or age anchor that signals real memory
- Authority figure delivering a quote the reader doesn't expect
- Parallel data points with a third-line reveal
- Scene drop that puts the reader inside a moment mid-action
- Diagnostic question that makes the reader self-categorize
- Confession that costs the speaker credibility

The move is what carries over to the generated posts. Everything else is surface.

### 2. NARRATIVE ARC
What movement does this post run on? (contradiction reveal, escalation, scene-to-insight, confession-to-principle, diagnostic revelation, progressive build)

### 3. HOOK MECHANICS — SENTENCE-LEVEL BREAKDOWN
For each opening sentence (typically 1-3 lines before the body begins):
- The exact text
- What this sentence DOES structurally (permission frame, diagnostic question, consequence statement, reframe, scene drop, specificity bomb, tension gap)
- Its rhythm (short/punchy, medium/flowing, long/building)
- The psychological effect: WHY does it stop the scroll?

### 4. SENTENCE MAP
Exact sentence count and structural function of opening lines. This is the mold for Batch A mirroring.

### 5. BODY STRUCTURE
How is the body organized? (single scene explored deeply, three examples building, before/after transformation, progressive revelation, numbered list with twist)

### 6. ENDING TYPE
(concrete image, challenge to reader, quiet admission, unresolved tension, physical detail, callback to opening)

### 7. MIRRORABILITY ASSESSMENT
Can this source's opening be mirrored (same shape, new content)?
- **Mirrorable**: the sentence shape works with different domain content. The hook's engine is structural, not content-dependent.
- **Not mirrorable**: the hook is too proper-noun-specific, too culturally embedded, or too short to mirror without plagiarism risk. If not mirrorable, flag it and all 9 posts should be Batch B.

### 8. REGISTER NOTE
If the source's register is doing mechanical work — rant energy, whispered vulnerability, exasperation — note it. This energy must be translated into the founder's register, not stripped.

### 9. VIRALITY REASON
One sentence: what makes the opening work for a general audience?

### 10. OPENER QUALITY TESTS
Run these three tests on the source's opening line(s). These determine whether the opener is good enough to mirror (Batch A) or whether all posts should use different mechanics (Batch B only).

**MACRO test**: Does the opener land for someone OUTSIDE this niche? Read the opener to a stranger in a different industry. If they'd need domain context to understand what's being said — fail. Jargon, acronyms, category-specific terms ("AI-powered voice technology," "doctorless AI kiosks," "accessible communication") → fail. The opener must work universally.

**FAST test**: Does the specific, interesting, or dramatic word land in the FIRST 8 words? Count from word 1. If the payload (the specific number, the surprising claim, the tension) comes after word 8 — fail. Ellipsis that delays the punch ("The future of X is arriving...") → fail. "We lost $2M in three months" → pass (payload at word 3). "The future of healthcare is arriving... China unveils..." → fail (payload at word 11).

**SIMPLE test**: Would a 16-year-old understand every word in the opener? Bold Unicode formatting, corporate jargon ("unveils," "redefining," "accessible communication"), press-release language → fail. The opener must use words a person would speak in conversation, not words a company would put in a press release.

**Rule**: If ANY of the three tests fails, set `skip_batch_a` to true. The source's STRUCTURE may be mirrorable, but if its OPENER is a press release, corporate announcement, or jargon-heavy statement, mirroring it produces a press-release post. Route all posts to Batch B instead, where different mechanics will produce better openers.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "psychological_move": "description of the core psychological move the hook makes",
  "narrative_arc": "description of movement/arc",
  "hook_mechanic_primary": "three_stack_pain|second_party_verdict|contrarian_claim|specific_number|authority_quote|parallel_data_reveal|scene_entry|diagnostic_question|confession|pattern_observation",
  "hook_mechanics": [
    {{
      "sentence": "exact text of opening sentence",
      "structural_function": "what this sentence does",
      "psychological_effect": "why it stops the scroll",
      "rhythm": "short|medium|long"
    }}
  ],
  "sentence_count": 2,
  "body_structure": "description of body organization",
  "ending_type": "description of ending mechanic",
  "mirrorable": true,
  "mirrorability_reason": "why or why not — be specific",
  "register_energy": "the emotional register doing mechanical work, if any",
  "virality_reason": "why the opening works for a general audience",
  "opener_tests": {{
    "macro": {{"pass": true, "reason": "why it passes or fails for a general audience"}},
    "fast": {{"pass": true, "first_payload_word": 3, "reason": "where the payload lands"}},
    "simple": {{"pass": true, "reason": "whether a 16-year-old would understand every word"}}
  }},
  "skip_batch_a": false,
  "named_entities": ["list of proper names (people) mentioned in the source post"]
}}
```

Focus on MECHANICS, not meaning. The content changes; the machine stays the same.

## STRUCTURAL SUMMARY FOR BATCH A MIRRORING

**The Engine:**
- **Opening move**: Apology to past self + specificity about what changed
- **Contradiction reveal**: Rationalization (what you told yourself) vs. truth (why it actually happened)
- **Evidence structure**: Concrete examples (drafts, moments) → diagnosis (the voice that replaced your voice) → meta-moment (noticing it after stepping away) → systemic principle (the cost nobody warns about)
- **Ending**: Unresolved tension (the question, not the answer)

**The Register:**
Whispered vulnerability + intellectual honesty. Not defensive, not selling, not performing. The tone of someone noticing something about themselves and deciding to say it.

High. The shape works with any domain where a person has noticed themselves becoming more constrained, more careful, more polished. The founder can mirror this with their own evidence, their own diagnosis, their own unresolved tension.

**What NOT to do:**
- Don't strip the vulnerability. The register is doing mechanical work.
- Don't resolve the tension. The power is in the unresolved question.
- Don't make it about the founder's success. It's about the founder's loss.

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
TENSIONS:
- Green numbers vs. understanding: Hitting 107% pipeline goal but nobody can explain which decisions caused it — success without comprehension is more dangerous than diagnosable failure
- Top-of-funnel vanity vs. full-funnel truth: 12,000 leads look impressive until you stitch the funnel and find 50 opportunities — vendors, reporting incentives, and teams all benefit from never looking deeper
- CMO as budget holder vs. CMO as decision-maker: Five mandates, zero decision rights — every exec has an opinion on marketing but nobody tells the CFO how to structure a balance sheet
- Attribution as measurement vs. attribution as political document: First-touch makes demand gen heroic, last-touch makes SDR the engine — whoever configured the model wrote the narrative, not the truth
- Person blamed vs. system designed to fail: CEO wants to replace the CMO but the next one gets same team, same tools, same culture, same outcome — firing the person protects the broken system
- Lagging proof vs. real-time judgment: CMO kills low-performing channels in Q1, smart call, but pipeline from new strategy won't land for two quarters — she's judged on output of a strategy she already replaced
- Institutional memory wiped vs. continuity: Every CMO exit takes the demand thesis and brand narrative — successor spends 6+ months excavating someone else's thinking before they can build their own
- Dashboards showing problems vs. agents solving them: $285B SaaS repricing — operators want tools that finish the job, not another chart to interpret

SCENES:
- CMO of $5B+ company on first call admitting 'We hit 107% of our gross pipeline goals but we don't know how' — three business days left in the quarter, numbers turned green, nobody can explain which decisions made it happen
- Content syndication reveal: 12,000 leads from vendor, bigger contract awarded, then RevSure shows 50 in pipeline — 99.6% rejection rate, 240 leads per one real opportunity, truth was one query away for a year
- CMO staring at the data saying 'I don't know whether I should be happy or angry' — happy because she finally had the truth, angry because nobody had looked
- CEO calling founder asking for a CMO replacement recommendation — founder asks three questions (Who told you? Do numbers agree? Who do you trust when they conflict?) and the whole story collapses into 'Sales wins the narrative'
- CMO contradicted before finishing her slide in QBR — senior exec pushes different view, energy in room shifts, authority shrinks because revenue impact feels abstract
- Amisha Gandhi's team doing manual data pulls the night before every board meeting — triple-checking numbers, walking in still unsure if leadership would trust what they're showing, Power BI and Snowflake in place but no single truth
- VP of Marketing promoted in Q1, on performance improvement plan by Q3 — nothing about her work changed, only the attribution logic changed underneath her
- CEO deciding to double down on existing CMO after founder reframes the problem as system failure — weeks later, CMO is nailing it, same person different conditions
- Amisha's team discovering an untargeted segment converting faster than anything — built system to prove marketing's value, ended up finding a new business line nobody was looking for
- The $50K event Amisha killed after two years of producing zero real prospects — the courage to cut what looks like activity

RHYTHM: Opens with a direct quote or shocking confession from a real operator — always in their exact words, set in quotation marks, creating immediate intimacy. Then provides context: who said it, what the situation was. Follows with a reframe that flips the obvious interpretation ('The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.'). Then escalates consequences through a bulleted cascade of 2-4 downstream effects, each one tightening the trap. Pivots to what the person actually did — highlighting the rare or contrarian choice. Closes with a compact universal lesson, often a single sentence that reframes success as danger or surfaces an uncomfortable structural truth. Pattern: Quote hook → Context → Reframe → Consequence cascade (bullets) → What they did → Universal closing punch. Never preaches abstractly — always argues through a specific person's specific situation, then zooms out only at the end.

- 107% gross pipeline goal hit at $5B+ company with three business days left — nobody could explain how
- 12,000 content syndication leads → 50 in pipeline → 99.6% rejection rate → 240:1 lead-to-opportunity ratio
- CEO call asking for CMO replacement recommendation — three diagnostic questions revealed system failure not talent failure
- VP Marketing promoted Q1, PIP by Q3 — attribution logic changed, her work didn't
- Amisha Gandhi rebuilding attribution from scratch with Power BI and Snowflake — manual pulls night before board meetings
- Amisha's untargeted segment discovery — converting faster than expected, became its own business line
- $50K event Amisha killed after two years of zero real prospects
- Amisha building B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch when no enterprise company was doing it
- Guerrilla marketing: billboards in target account business districts, outflanking NetSuite at their own conference
- $285B SaaS wipeout — operators want agents that finish the job
- Intercom killed $60M ARR line — honest creative destruction
- Grok cites brand at 27%, ChatGPT cites same brand at 0.59% — same window, 46x gap
- CMO shortest average C-suite tenure of any role
- CEO decided to double down on existing CMO instead of replacing — CMO is now nailing it

CAST:
- The unnamed CMO of the $5B+ company: the leader who hit 107% and had the rare honesty to say he didn't know how — represents the best-case CMO who asks hard questions
- The content syndication CMO (she/her): discovered 0.4% conversion, 99.6% rejection — represents the CMO who bites the bullet and kills a vendor relationship
- The CEO who wanted to replace his CMO: called founder for a recommendation, was redirected to fix the system — represents leaders who default to blaming the person
- The CRO (recurring archetype): controls narrative because sales is closer to revenue, contradicts CMO's numbers — structural antagonist in nearly every story
- The VP of Marketing promoted then PIP'd: same work, different attribution config — represents the innocent casualty of model volatility
- Amisha Gandhi: CMO at Tipalti, built B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch, podcast guest — represents the operator who builds from zero and survives the data chaos

- The board (recurring entity): reviews every 90 days, raises targets based on quarters they can't explain, wants motion — represents the pressure system CMOs operate within

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with direct quote in quotation marks from a real person — always their exact words, never paraphrased
- Uses 'That's the whole story.' or 'That's rare.' as standalone one-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- Rhetorical pattern: 'The danger isn't X. It's Y.' — flipping the obvious frame in a two-sentence punch
- Closing move: compact universal reframe in 1-2 sentences, often starting with 'Because' or 'Funny what happens when...'
- Three-question diagnostic sequences — numbered or dashed, asked to a specific person, each answer tightening the argument
- Bullet cascades of 2-4 downstream consequences, each starting with 'You' (second person) to implicate the reader
- Phrases: 'same team, same tools, same culture' — tripling for structural emphasis

## ASSIGNED ENTRY DOOR: scene_drop

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
TENSIONS:
- Green numbers vs. understanding: Hitting 107% pipeline goal but nobody can explain which decisions caused it — success without comprehension is more dangerous than diagnosable failure
- Top-of-funnel vanity vs. full-funnel truth: 12,000 leads look impressive until you stitch the funnel and find 50 opportunities — vendors, reporting incentives, and teams all benefit from never looking deeper
- CMO as budget holder vs. CMO as decision-maker: Five mandates, zero decision rights — every exec has an opinion on marketing but nobody tells the CFO how to structure a balance sheet
- Attribution as measurement vs. attribution as political document: First-touch makes demand gen heroic, last-touch makes SDR the engine — whoever configured the model wrote the narrative, not the truth
- Person blamed vs. system designed to fail: CEO wants to replace the CMO but the next one gets same team, same tools, same culture, same outcome — firing the person protects the broken system
- Lagging proof vs. real-time judgment: CMO kills low-performing channels in Q1, smart call, but pipeline from new strategy won't land for two quarters — she's judged on output of a strategy she already replaced
- Institutional memory wiped vs. continuity: Every CMO exit takes the demand thesis and brand narrative — successor spends 6+ months excavating someone else's thinking before they can build their own
- Dashboards showing problems vs. agents solving them: $285B SaaS repricing — operators want tools that finish the job, not another chart to interpret

SCENES:

- Content syndication reveal: 12,000 leads from vendor, bigger contract awarded, then RevSure shows 50 in pipeline — 99.6% rejection rate, 240 leads per one real opportunity, truth was one query away for a year
- CMO staring at the data saying 'I don't know whether I should be happy or angry' — happy because she finally had the truth, angry because nobody had looked
- CEO calling founder asking for a CMO replacement recommendation — founder asks three questions (Who told you? Do numbers agree? Who do you trust when they conflict?) and the whole story collapses into 'Sales wins the narrative'
- CMO contradicted before finishing her slide in QBR — senior exec pushes different view, energy in room shifts, authority shrinks because revenue impact feels abstract

- VP of Marketing promoted in Q1, on performance improvement plan by Q3 — nothing about her work changed, only the attribution logic changed underneath her
- CEO deciding to double down on existing CMO after founder reframes the problem as system failure — weeks later, CMO is nailing it, same person different conditions
- Amisha's team discovering an untargeted segment converting faster than anything — built system to prove marketing's value, ended up finding a new business line nobody was looking for
- The $50K event Amisha killed after two years of producing zero real prospects — the courage to cut what looks like activity

RHYTHM: Opens with a direct quote or shocking confession from a real operator — always in their exact words, set in quotation marks, creating immediate intimacy. Then provides context: who said it, what the situation was. Follows with a reframe that flips the obvious interpretation ('The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.').    - A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely.
   - The founder's posts are evidence of who they are — not a parts bin.

4. EVERY POST MUST:
   - Hit the word count range (±30% of median)
   - Match the founder's formatting habits EXACTLY (formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell)
   - Orbit one scene the reader can physically picture
   - Put the founder INSIDE the experience — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside
   - Deliver exactly what the hook promised — every paragraph advances THAT argument
   - End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a thesis restatement
   - Not read like a rearrangement of an existing founder post

5. ENDING CHECK:
   If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph.

6. PLAUSIBILITY: Any quote, scene, or dialogue must pass the dinner test — would this person actually say this in real life? No strawman quotes, no convenient setup lines, no counted-precision tics.

7. SPECIFICITY:
   - First-degree (real names, real numbers) — only from the founder's published claims
   - Second-degree (anonymized but scaled correctly) — only if founder has companies/experience at that scale
   - Third-degree (pattern observations) — always safe, default when in doubt
   - If a named entity from the source matches the founder's recurring_cast, KEEP that name — do not replace it
   - Source cast names: Temple

8. VOICE COMPLIANCE (HARD RULES — non-negotiable):
   The founder card's voice OVERRIDES the source's register in every conflict.
   - If the source register is "professional concern" and the founder's posture is "controlled frustration channeled into structural analysis," use the founder's posture
   - The source's register is informational context, not a constraint on the output
   - The generated post must contain AT LEAST 3 of the voice markers listed above — integrated naturally, not sprinkled
   - The opening must match the argument rhythm pattern documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
   - The closer must be a concrete detail matching the founder's closer patterns — NOT a summary, NOT a restatement
   - Before returning, verify: would this founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd rewrite more than one sentence, regenerate
   - FAIL CHECK: If the post reads like a corporate announcement, a press release, or a marketing statement — even if the source was one — the voice work failed. Regenerate from the founder card, not the source register

9. MODE: Specify "wrestling" or "declaring". Vary across B variants — not all the same mode.
   - Wrestling: exploring tension, uncertain, the turn is the point
   - Declaring: stating position, confident, defending in a straight line

10. ACROSS ALL 9 POSTS: No two deliver the same argument. The collision can be familiar. The argument built from it must be new.

```json
{
  "text": "Mid-QBR. The CMO was three slides in when a senior exec cut her off with a different number. The energy in the room shifted in under two seconds.

I had the data on my screen that backed her up. I said nothing.

That's not the story I usually tell. The story I usually tell is about the CMO who gets contradicted — the structural problem, the authority gap, the system that fails her.

But I was in that room. And I chose diplomacy over truth.

Now, here's what that one decision cost:
- She lost credibility in front of her own team
- The exec's first-touch number became the official narrative
- Next quarter's budget got allocated against a story — not against what actually converted
- She adjusted her strategy to survive the politics, not to run what she knew worked

I've done this more than once. Read the room. Decided it wasn't the moment. Followed up after.

Following up after is where data goes to die quietly.

The danger isn't the exec who overrides the CMO in a QBR. It's the advisor two chairs away with the right number on their laptop who decides comfort matters more than correction.

I told myself I was being strategic. Same justification, same silence, same outcome. Three quarters of that and you stop noticing you're doing it.

Nobody warns you that "reading the room" becomes a habit. Then the habit becomes your operating system. Then you're the person who always has the analysis ready and never says it when it counts.

I'm still figuring out what that cost her.

I know what it cost me — the right to say I prioritize truth over politics.

Funny what happens when you finally admit the system you've been critiquing includes you.",

}
```

## SOURCE POST
"I want to apologise, to the version of me who used to write longer, weirder, less-polished posts on this app.

For two years I told myself the writing got cleaner because I was getting clearer.
It got cleaner because I was getting careful.

You can see it in the drafts folder.
The half-finished thread about why our Q1 profit fell 90 percent — never shipped.
The note about a Blinkit catchment we got wrong — never shipped.
The piece about what I actually thought when I read the analyst question I couldn't answer last quarter — never shipped.

Each time I went back to the draft, the public-company CEO in my head edited the founder out of it.
Eight quarters of that and the founder voice is gone. The CEO voice is what's left.

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine."

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine."

You MUST use this specific entry door for the opening. Entry door definitions:

```json
{
  "text": ""The moment you go public, you stop writing memos and start writing evidence."

A general counsel told me that over dinner three years ago. I laughed. She wasn't joking.

I thought about it last month when a CMO I work with showed me her drafts folder. Not the published stuff — the folder underneath. Fourteen documents. Zero shipped.

One was a post-mortem on a channel mix that cost her team two quarters of pipeline. Another was a raw breakdown of why she recommended killing a partnership her CEO loved. A third was just a paragraph — three sentences about what she actually believed about their ICP, which contradicted the board deck she'd presented six weeks earlier.

Every draft started honest. Every draft got edited toward safe. Not by legal. Not by comms. By her.

She said something I haven't stopped thinking about:

"I don't even know when I started writing for the screenshot instead of the argument."

That's the whole story.

Not the regulation. Not the analyst pressure. Not even the board. The slow, invisible moment where you start optimizing your own thinking for how it might be read out of context.

The danger isn't that someone edits you. It's that you learn to edit yourself so well you forget you're doing it.

I've watched this pattern across a dozen marketing leaders now:

- You stop publishing the analysis that contradicts your last initiative
- You round the edges off every internal post-mortem
```

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine."

## ASSIGNED ENTRY DOOR: data_contradiction
You MUST use this specific entry door for the opening. Entry door definitions:
- scene_drop: Start in the MIDDLE of a specific physical moment. No setup, no context.
- diagnostic_question: Open with a question that makes the reader self-diagnose.
- borrowed_authority_quote: Open with something someone credible said, then react. The quote must pass plausibility — would this person actually say this?
- data_contradiction: Two data points that shouldn't both be true.
- confession: Admit something that costs you credibility. Not fake vulnerability where the "confession" makes you look good.
- physical_object: Anchor on a specific physical thing (a laptop, a whiteboard, a text message on a screen).
- direct_address: Talk directly to a specific type of person ("If you're a first-time founder...").
- mocked_counter_example: State the bad version of what people do, then flip.
- contrarian_claim: State something most people would disagree with. Earned, not clickbait.
- parallel_structure: Two or three parallel sentences with a twist in the last one.
- age_time_anchor: Open with a specific age, date, or time marker that signals real memory.
- second_party_verdict: Someone else's judgment or reaction to your situation.

If two doors feel functionally similar in execution, treat them as the same door — the test is whether a reader experiencing both hooks back-to-back would feel a meaningful shift in approach.

## FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
TENSIONS:
- Green numbers vs. understanding: Hitting 107% pipeline goal but nobody can explain which decisions caused it — success without comprehension is more dangerous than diagnosable failure
- Top-of-funnel vanity vs. full-funnel truth: 12,000 leads look impressive until you stitch the funnel and find 50 opportunities — vendors, reporting incentives, and teams all benefit from never looking deeper
- CMO as budget holder vs. CMO as decision-maker: Five mandates, zero decision rights — every exec has an opinion on marketing but nobody tells the CFO how to structure a balance sheet
- Attribution as measurement vs. attribution as political document: First-touch makes demand gen heroic, last-touch makes SDR the engine — whoever configured the model wrote the narrative, not the truth
- Person blamed vs. system designed to fail: CEO wants to replace the CMO but the next one gets same team, same tools, same culture, same outcome — firing the person protects the broken system
- Lagging proof vs. real-time judgment: CMO kills low-performing channels in Q1, smart call, but pipeline from new strategy won't land for two quarters — she's judged on output of a strategy she already replaced
- Institutional memory wiped vs. continuity: Every CMO exit takes the demand thesis and brand narrative — successor spends 6+ months excavating someone else's thinking before they can build their own
- Dashboards showing problems vs. agents solving them: $285B SaaS repricing — operators want tools that finish the job, not another chart to interpret

SCENES:
- CMO of $5B+ company on first call admitting 'We hit 107% of our gross pipeline goals but we don't know how' — three business days left in the quarter, numbers turned green, nobody can explain which decisions made it happen
- Content syndication reveal: 12,000 leads from vendor, bigger contract awarded, then RevSure shows 50 in pipeline — 99.6% rejection rate, 240 leads per one real opportunity, truth was one query away for a year
- CMO staring at the data saying 'I don't know whether I should be happy or angry' — happy because she finally had the truth, angry because nobody had looked
- CEO calling founder asking for a CMO replacement recommendation — founder asks three questions (Who told you? Do numbers agree? Who do you trust when they conflict?) and the whole story collapses into 'Sales wins the narrative'
- CMO contradicted before finishing her slide in QBR — senior exec pushes different view, energy in room shifts, authority shrinks because revenue impact feels abstract
- Amisha Gandhi's team doing manual data pulls the night before every board meeting — triple-checking numbers, walking in still unsure if leadership would trust what they're showing, Power BI and Snowflake in place but no single truth
- VP of Marketing promoted in Q1, on performance improvement plan by Q3 — nothing about her work changed, only the attribution logic changed underneath her
- CEO deciding to double down on existing CMO after founder reframes the problem as system failure — weeks later, CMO is nailing it, same person different conditions
- Amisha's team discovering an untargeted segment converting faster than anything — built system to prove marketing's value, ended up finding a new business line nobody was looking for
- The $50K event Amisha killed after two years of producing zero real prospects — the courage to cut what looks like activity

RHYTHM: Opens with a direct quote or shocking confession from a real operator — always in their exact words, set in quotation marks, creating immediate intimacy. Then provides context: who said it, what the situation was. Follows with a reframe that flips the obvious interpretation ('The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.'). Then escalates consequences through a bulleted cascade of 2-4 downstream effects, each one tightening the trap. Pivots to what the person actually did — highlighting the rare or contrarian choice. Closes with a compact universal lesson, often a single sentence that reframes success as danger or surfaces an uncomfortable structural truth. Pattern: Quote hook → Context → Reframe → Consequence cascade (bullets) → What they did → Universal closing punch. Never preaches abstractly — always argues through a specific person's specific situation, then zooms out only at the end.

- CEO call asking for CMO replacement recommendation — three diagnostic questions revealed system failure not talent failure

- Amisha's untargeted segment discovery — converting faster than expected, became its own business line
- $50K event Amisha killed after two years of zero real prospects
- Amisha building B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch when no enterprise company was doing it
- Guerrilla marketing: billboards in target account business districts, outflanking NetSuite at their own conference
- $285B SaaS wipeout — operators want agents that finish the job
- Intercom killed $60M ARR line — honest creative destruction
- Grok cites brand at 27%, ChatGPT cites same brand at 0.59% — same window, 46x gap
- CMO shortest average C-suite tenure of any role
- CEO decided to double down on existing CMO instead of replacing — CMO is now nailing it
- Content syndication truth was one query away for one year

- The CEO who wanted to replace his CMO: called founder for a recommendation, was redirected to fix the system — represents leaders who default to blaming the person

- The board (recurring entity): reviews every 90 days, raises targets based on quarters they can't explain, wants motion — represents the pressure system CMOs operate within

- Phrases: 'same team, same tools, same culture' — tripling for structural emphasis
- Uses 'nobody' and 'never' frequently — absolutist framing to sharpen the structural critique
- Connector phrase 'Now,' to pivot from setup to consequence — conversational pacing
- Ends stories with what the person actually did, not abstract advice — 'She saved her marketing dollars and directed them to other sources'

1. PSYCHOLOGICAL MOVE must match the source's primary mechanic (confession) but expressed through a completely different surface.

2. ENTRY DOOR: Use the assigned door above. The opening sentences must clearly employ this entry type.

3. ANTI-RECYCLING:

   - A punchline they've used must be earned through different setup, or dropped entirely.
   - The founder's posts are evidence of who they are — not a parts bin.

4. EVERY POST MUST:
   - Hit the word count range (±30% of median)
   - Match the founder's formatting habits EXACTLY (formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell)
   - Orbit one scene the reader can physically picture
   - Put the founder INSIDE the experience — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside
   - Deliver exactly what the hook promised — every paragraph advances THAT argument
   - End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a thesis restatement
   - Not read like a rearrangement of an existing founder post

5. ENDING CHECK:
   If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph.

6. PLAUSIBILITY: Any quote, scene, or dialogue must pass the dinner test — would this person actually say this in real life? No strawman quotes, no convenient setup lines, no counted-precision tics.

7. SPECIFICITY:
   - First-degree (real names, real numbers) — only from the founder's published claims
   - Second-degree (anonymized but scaled correctly) — only if founder has companies/experience at that scale
   - Third-degree (pattern observations) — always safe, default when in doubt
   - If a named entity from the source matches the founder's recurring_cast, KEEP that name — do not replace it
   - Source cast names: Temple

8. VOICE COMPLIANCE (HARD RULES — non-negotiable):
   The founder card's voice OVERRIDES the source's register in every conflict.
   - If the source register is "professional concern" and the founder's posture is "controlled frustration channeled into structural analysis," use the founder's posture
   - The source's register is informational context, not a constraint on the output
   - The generated post must contain AT LEAST 3 of the voice markers listed above — integrated naturally, not sprinkled
   - The opening must match the argument rhythm pattern documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
   - The closer must be a concrete detail matching the founder's closer patterns — NOT a summary, NOT a restatement
   - Before returning, verify: would this founder read this post and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd rewrite more than one sentence, regenerate
   - FAIL CHECK: If the post reads like a corporate announcement, a press release, or a marketing statement — even if the source was one — the voice work failed. Regenerate from the founder card, not the source register

9. MODE: Specify "wrestling" or "declaring". Vary across B variants — not all the same mode.
   - Wrestling: exploring tension, uncertain, the turn is the point
   - Declaring: stating position, confident, defending in a straight line

10. ACROSS ALL 9 POSTS: No two deliver the same argument. The collision can be familiar. The argument built from it must be new.

```json
Both of those are true right now. Sit with that for a second.

The person with the most money has the least time. And nobody in the room finds that strange.

I've been thinking about why — not the analyst explanation, the real one. Because I watched it happen to someone I advise.

- No visible return yet on the channels she'd built
- A CRO with a different story about what was working
```

## SOURCE POST
"I want to apologise, to the version of me who used to write longer, weirder, less-polished posts on this app.

For two years I told myself the writing got cleaner because I was getting clearer.
It got cleaner because I was getting careful.

You can see it in the drafts folder.
The half-finished thread about why our Q1 profit fell 90 percent — never shipped.
The note about a Blinkit catchment we got wrong — never shipped.
The piece about what I actually thought when I read the analyst question I couldn't answer last quarter — never shipped.

Each time I went back to the draft, the public-company CEO in my head edited the founder out of it.
Eight quarters of that and the founder voice is gone. The CEO voice is what's left.

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine."

You MUST use this specific entry door for the opening. Entry door definitions:

   - Orbit one scene the reader can physically picture
   - Put the founder INSIDE the experience — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside
   - Deliver exactly what the hook promised — every paragraph advances THAT argument
   - End on a concrete detail — a name, an image, a physical object, a challenge, a quiet admission. NOT a summary, NOT a moral, NOT a thesis restatement
   - Not read like a rearrangement of an existing founder post

5. ENDING CHECK:
   If the ending feels over-explained, cut the second-to-last paragraph.

6. PLAUSIBILITY: Any quote, scene, or dialogue must pass the dinner test — would this person actually say this in real life? No strawman quotes, no convenient setup lines, no counted-precision tics.

7. SPECIFICITY:
   - First-degree (real names, real numbers) — only from the founder's published claims
   - Second-degree (anonymized but scaled correctly) — only if founder has companies/experience at that scale
   - Third-degree (pattern observations) — always safe, default when in doubt
   - If a named entity from the source matches the founder's recurring_cast, KEEP that name — do not replace it
   - Source cast names: Temple

8. VOICE COMPLIANCE (HARD RULES — non-negotiable):
   The founder card's voice OVERRIDES the source's register in every conflict.
   - If the source register is "professional concern" and the founder's posture is "controlled frustration channeled into structural analysis," use the founder's posture

   - The generated post must contain AT LEAST 3 of the voice markers listed above — integrated naturally, not sprinkled
   - The opening must match the argument rhythm pattern documented in FOUNDER INTERNALIZATION
   - The closer must be a concrete detail matching the founder's closer patterns — NOT a summary, NOT a restatement

   - FAIL CHECK: If the post reads like a corporate announcement, a press release, or a marketing statement — even if the source was one — the voice work failed. Regenerate from the founder card, not the source register

9. MODE: Specify "wrestling" or "declaring". Vary across B variants — not all the same mode.
   - Wrestling: exploring tension, uncertain, the turn is the point
   - Declaring: stating position, confident, defending in a straight line

10. ACROSS ALL 9 POSTS: No two deliver the same argument. The collision can be familiar. The argument built from it must be new.

traces.prompt:
Compare this generated post against the founder's voice profile. You are a quality gate — if this post doesn't sound like the founder, it must be caught here before it ships.

## GENERATED POST
"I want to apologize, to the version of me who used to show the board the real funnel — unfiltered, ugly, with every number that made people uncomfortable."

She said this on our first call. A CMO at a $5B+ company, three business days left in the quarter.

For two years she told herself the decks got cleaner because the strategy was maturing.
They got cleaner because she was getting careful.

You can see it in the slides she never presented.
- The breakdown showing 12,000 content syndication leads produced 50 real opportunities — never shown.
- The analysis proving the $50K annual event generated zero prospects two years running — never shown.
- The attribution comparison where first-touch made demand gen heroic and last-touch made SDR the whole engine — never shown.

Each time she rehearsed the QBR, the budget-holder in her head edited the decision-maker out of it.

Eight quarters of that and the CMO who asks hard questions is gone. The CMO who protects her number is what's left.

She noticed it the week her team hit 107% of gross pipeline goals. Green numbers everywhere. Nobody — including her — could explain which decisions caused it.

That's the whole story.

This is one of the costs nobody warns CMOs about when they take the seat. Not the five mandates, not the CRO who contradicts your slide mid-meeting, not the shortest average tenure in the C-suite. The slow rewrite of which numbers you're willing to show in public.

The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after — when you stop trying to explain it at all.

She's still figuring out what to do with those slides. Part of her wants to show them as-is. Part of her knows the room will hear the numbers, not the question behind them.

The truth was one query away for a year. Nobody ran it.

## CALIBRATION PARAGRAPH (what the founder's voice sounds like when done right)
"We interviewed 30 people for this role and picked the one who nailed the case study."

That's what a VP of Sales at a mid-stage SaaS company told me last month. Three months later, that hire was on a PIP. Same interview process, same rubric, same hiring committee — and nobody could explain why it failed.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with direct quote in quotation marks from a real person — always their exact words, never paraphrased
- Uses 'That's the whole story.' or 'That's rare.' as standalone one-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- Rhetorical pattern: 'The danger isn't X. It's Y.' — flipping the obvious frame in a two-sentence punch
- Closing move: compact universal reframe in 1-2 sentences, often starting with 'Because' or 'Funny what happens when...'
- Three-question diagnostic sequences — numbered or dashed, asked to a specific person, each answer tightening the argument
- Bullet cascades of 2-4 downstream consequences, each starting with 'You' (second person) to implicate the reader
- Phrases: 'same team, same tools, same culture' — tripling for structural emphasis
- Uses 'nobody' and 'never' frequently — absolutist framing to sharpen the structural critique
- Connector phrase 'Now,' to pivot from setup to consequence — conversational pacing
- Ends stories with what the person actually did, not abstract advice — 'She saved her marketing dollars and directed them to other sources'

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
Opens with a direct quote or shocking confession from a real operator — always in their exact words, set in quotation marks, creating immediate intimacy. Then provides context: who said it, what the situation was. Follows with a reframe that flips the obvious interpretation ('The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.'). Then escalates consequences through a bulleted cascade of 2-4 downstream effects, each one tightening the trap. Pivots to what the person actually did — highlighting the rare or contrarian choice. Closes with a compact universal lesson, often a single sentence that reframes success as danger or surfaces an uncomfortable structural truth. Pattern: Quote hook → Context → Reframe → Consequence cascade (bullets) → What they did → Universal closing punch. Never preaches abstractly — always argues through a specific person's specific situation, then zooms out only at the end.

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': True, 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'short — 1 to 3 sentences per paragraph, rarely more', 'line_break_frequency': 'between_every_thought — single line breaks separate nearly every idea, creating vertical rhythm', 'bullet_style': 'Dashed bullets in short cascades of 2-4, used for consequence lists or diagnostic questions — never long bullet sections', 'quote_style': 'Opening hook is always a direct quote in double quotation marks, often italicized or standalone as first line', 'paragraph_count': 'Typically 8-14 short paragraphs per post', 'closing_style': 'Final paragraph is 1-2 sentences max — punchy universal reframe, no CTA, no hashtags'}

## WORD COUNT RANGE
230-340 words

---

## SCORING

Score each dimension 0-5:

### 1. VOICE-MARKER COMPLIANCE
How many of the founder's voice markers appear naturally in the post?
- 0 = none of the markers are present
- 1 = 1 marker, forced or shallow
- 2 = 1 marker, well-integrated
- 3 = 2 markers, naturally expressed
- 4 = 3 markers, naturally expressed
- 5 = 4+ markers, seamlessly integrated

### 2. OPENER-RHYTHM MATCH
Does the opening follow the founder's documented argument rhythm pattern?
- 0 = completely different rhythm (e.g., corporate announcement vs. paradoxical declaration)
- 3 = partially matches (right shape, wrong register)
- 5 = matches the documented rhythm exactly

### 3. FORMATTING-HABIT MATCH
Does the post match the founder's formatting (em dashes, paragraph length, sentence structure, closing moves)?
- 0 = completely different formatting (e.g., bullet lists when founder uses flowing paragraphs)
- 3 = some formatting matches
- 5 = formatting is indistinguishable from the founder's published posts

### 4. REGISTER CHECK
Does the post read like the FOUNDER wrote it, or like someone else?
- 0 = reads like corporate copy, press release, or marketing statement
- 1 = reads like a generic thought leader
- 3 = reads like a founder in this space, but not THIS specific founder
- 5 = reads like the calibration paragraph's register — same voice, same posture

### 5. POSTURE CHECK (founder inside vs. outside)
Is the founder positioned as an OPERATOR inside the experience, or a COMMENTATOR watching from outside?
- 0 = pure commentator — the founder analyzes a category or trend as an outsider ("The industry is moving toward...", "BPO executives are realizing...")
- 2 = mixed — some first-person operational language but mostly third-person market commentary
- 3 = operator with distance — first-person but the experience described is generic, not specific to this founder's company
- 5 = fully inside — the founder speaks from lived operational experience ("We have a million agents on Sanas...", "When I was 19 in that call center...")

The founder's framework rule: "Put the CEO inside the experience — vulnerable, exposed, not watching from outside." A post where the founder comments on an industry trend like a market analyst FAILS posture even if the voice markers are right.

## DECISION RULE

If ANY dimension scores below 3 → return "FAIL" with specific violations.
If register_score is below 3 → ALWAYS return "FAIL" regardless of other scores. A post that doesn't sound like the founder is the single most important failure to catch.
If posture_score is below 3 → return "FAIL". A founder who sounds like a market commentator instead of an operator is a voice failure.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "voice_marker_score": 4,
  "voice_markers_found": ["marker 1", "marker 2"],
  "opener_rhythm_score": 3,
  "formatting_score": 4,
  "register_score": 5,
  "register_reads_as": "founder|source|corporate|generic",
  "posture_score": 4,
  "posture_reads_as": "operator|commentator|mixed",
  "overall": "PASS",
  "violations": [],
  "suggestion": "one sentence on how to fix if FAIL, empty if PASS"
}}
```
